pois.name: Highwater Eatery
pois.short: Upscale Cuba Street restaurant — sustainability-focused with exposed brick, inventive tasting menus.
pois.commentary: Highwater sits at the top of Cuba Street and sets the bar for sustainable fine dining in Wellington. The tasting menus change with the seasons, sourcing from regenerative farms and ethical fisheries. The exposed brick and industrial-chic interior create an atmosphere that's refined without being stuffy. The wine list champions small NZ producers, and the cocktail programme is equally thoughtful.

reviews.summary: The sustainability commitment impresses without being preachy. The tasting menus are creative and well-priced for the quality. Some find the portion sizes small, but the multi-course structure satisfies.

pois.name: Flight Coffee Hangar
pois.short: Wellington's top specialty coffee roaster — the Dixon Street hangar is a pilgrimage site for flat-white lovers.
pois.commentary: Flight Coffee has been at the forefront of Wellington's specialty coffee scene since 2012. The 'Hangar' on Dixon Street is part roastery, part café — you can watch beans being roasted while sipping what many consider the best flat white in town. Their single-origin filter coffees rotate regularly, and the baristas are passionate about walking you through the offerings. No fancy food — just perfect coffee.

reviews.summary: Coffee nerds call Flight the best in Wellington. The roastery experience adds to the visit. Limited food and seating noted as minor drawbacks.

pois.name: ParrotDog Brewery
pois.short: Lyall Bay brewery and taproom — core range of excellent beers plus adventurous limited releases.
pois.commentary: ParrotDog's Lyall Bay brewery is a big, industrial space with a lively taproom and beer garden. The core range (Bitterbitch IPA, Bloodhound Red Ale) is solid, but it's the limited releases and barrel-aged specials that draw beer geeks. The taproom serves good food — burgers, tacos, sharing plates — and there's usually something happening on weekends. A short walk from Lyall Bay beach.

reviews.summary: Beer lovers appreciate the range and quality. The industrial-chic space is praised. The Lyall Bay location is a bit out of the way for tourists, but worth the trip.

pois.name: Wellington Harbourside Market
pois.short: Sunday morning waterfront market — 60+ stalls of local produce, street food and artisan goods.
pois.commentary: Every Sunday morning the Wellington waterfront comes alive with one of New Zealand's longest-running farmers' markets. Over 60 stallholders sell seasonal produce, artisan breads, local cheeses, organic meats and prepared foods. The street-food stalls (Turkish gözleme, fresh crêpes, dumplings) make it a breakfast destination. Arrive early — the best produce sells out by mid-morning.

reviews.summary: A Wellington institution that locals swear by. The variety and quality are excellent. Gets very crowded by 10am — early birds get the best experience.

pois.name: Ombra
pois.short: Venetian-style bacaro on Cuba Street — Italian small plates, natural wines and a buzzy corner atmosphere.
pois.commentary: Ombra channels the bacari of Venice — small, convivial wine bars where you graze on cicchetti (Venetian bar snacks) over a glass of something interesting. The menu is Italian through and through, with handmade pasta, cured meats and seasonal vegetables, all paired with a carefully curated natural wine list. The corner location on Cuba Street makes for excellent people-watching. No bookings for small parties — just turn up.

reviews.summary: The authentic Italian small-plates concept wins loyal fans. The natural wine list is praised. No-bookings policy means waits on busy evenings.

pois.name: Moore Wilson's Fresh
pois.short: Wellington institution — part specialty grocer, part fresh market, and the city's best food shopping experience.
pois.commentary: Moore Wilson's is where Wellington's chefs and food lovers shop. The Fresh section is essentially an indoor food market — local cheeses, charcuterie, artisan breads, fresh produce, specialty oils and vinegars. The bulk wine section has an excellent selection at good prices. Even if you're not cooking, it's worth visiting to understand why Wellington considers itself the food capital of New Zealand.

reviews.summary: Wellingtonians are fiercely loyal to Moore Wilson's. The fresh produce and specialty items are praised. Can feel overwhelming for first-time visitors — the regulars know exactly where everything is.

pois.name: Greytown Food & Wine Precinct
pois.short: Charming Victorian village in the Wairarapa — boutique wineries, artisan chocolate and destination dining.
pois.commentary: Greytown is the prettiest village in the Wairarapa and its food scene rivals towns five times its size. The main street is lined with cafés, restaurants and specialty food shops — the artisan chocolate at Schoc, the French pastries at C'est Cheese, the seasonal tasting menus at various restaurants. Nearby wineries (Palliser, Ata Rangi, Dry River) are a short drive. It makes a perfect day trip from Wellington, especially combined with Martinborough.

reviews.summary: The village charm combined with quality food impresses. Schoc chocolate is a must-buy. Some find it very busy on weekends and quiet midweek.

pois.name: Ata Rangi Winery
pois.short: Martinborough's legendary Pinot Noir producer — one of NZ's original and finest.
pois.commentary: Clive Paton planted Ata Rangi's first Pinot Noir vines in Martinborough in 1980 using cuttings reportedly traced to Domaine de la Romanée-Conti in Burgundy. Today, Ata Rangi is one of New Zealand's most revered Pinot Noir producers. The cellar door in Martinborough's wine village offers tastings by appointment, and the wines consistently earn five stars from NZ critics. A pilgrimage for Pinot lovers.

reviews.summary: NZ wine critics consistently rank Ata Rangi among the country's best. The DRC connection story adds mystique. By-appointment only — book ahead.

pois.name: Palliser Estate
pois.short: Martinborough estate with an outstanding cellar door — Pinot Noir, Sauvignon Blanc and seasonal food pairings.
pois.commentary: Palliser is one of Martinborough's largest and most visitor-friendly wineries. The modern cellar door offers tastings across their full range — the Pinot Noir and Sauvignon Blanc are both excellent — and seasonal food-and-wine pairings are available on weekends. The estate also produces an outstanding méthode traditionnelle sparkling. Easier to visit spontaneously than some of the smaller Martinborough producers.

reviews.summary: The cellar door experience is praised as welcoming and well-organised. The wines offer great value. Some find it less intimate than smaller neighbours, but the quality is reliable.

pois.name: Dry River Wines
pois.short: Cult Martinborough winery — tiny production, legendary Pinot Gris and Gewürztraminer, by appointment only.
pois.commentary: Founded by the late Neil McCallum in 1979, Dry River has achieved almost cult status among NZ wine enthusiasts. The aromatic whites — particularly the Pinot Gris and Gewürztraminer — are among the finest in the country, and the Pinot Noir ages beautifully. Production is tiny and most wine sells to the mailing list, so a cellar door visit (by appointment) may be your only chance to taste them.

reviews.summary: Wine enthusiasts consider Dry River essential Wairarapa. The aromatic whites are called world-class. The appointment-only, small-production model means availability can be limited.

pois.name: Scopa Pizzeria
pois.short: Wellington's best pizza — wood-fired Neapolitan-style in a buzzy Cuba Street setting.
pois.commentary: Scopa has held its own in Wellington's fiercely competitive pizza scene for years. The Neapolitan-style pies are made with imported Italian flour and San Marzano tomatoes, fired in a blistering wood oven, and served in a lively Cuba Street dining room. The Margherita is the test — and Scopa passes with flying colours. Good Italian wine list and cocktails, too. No bookings — arrive early or prepare to wait.

reviews.summary: Consistently rated Wellington's best pizza. The authentic Neapolitan style impresses. No-bookings policy means queues on weekend evenings.

pois.name: Wellington on a Plate Festival
pois.short: NZ's biggest food festival — two weeks in August with pop-ups, dinners, burger week and Visa Wellington on a Plate.
pois.commentary: Wellington on a Plate is New Zealand's premier food festival, running for two weeks each August. The festival encompasses Burger Wellington (where over 100 venues create unique competition burgers), craft beer events, pop-up restaurants, winemaker dinners and culinary experiences across the city and Wairarapa. It's the event that earned Wellington its 'food capital' reputation. Individual events are separately ticketed.

reviews.summary: Foodies from across NZ make the pilgrimage for this festival. Burger Wellington is the crowd favourite. Some events sell out weeks ahead — book early for the best experiences.

pois.name: Regional Wines & Spirits
pois.short: Wellington's premier wine shop and tasting bar — NZ-focused with knowledgeable staff and regular tastings.
pois.commentary: Regional is part bottle shop, part wine bar, and entirely staffed by wine obsessives. The selection focuses on New Zealand and Australia, with deep representation from smaller producers you won't find in supermarkets. Regular tasting events showcase particular regions or winemakers, and the staff are genuinely helpful rather than intimidating. It's where Wellington's wine industry drinks on its nights off.

reviews.summary: Wine lovers praise the knowledgeable staff and curated selection. The tasting events are popular and well-run. The small space can feel crowded during events.

pois.name: Hippopotamus Restaurant
pois.short: Wellington's French fine-dining institution — the degustation at Museum Hotel is a special-occasion classic.
pois.commentary: Hippopotamus has been Wellington's premier French-inspired fine dining destination for years, set in the Museum Hotel on the waterfront. The degustation menus are meticulously crafted, with NZ produce given French technique and flair. The wine list is extensive and the sommelier service is among the best in the country. This is where Wellington celebrates — engagements, anniversaries, career milestones.

reviews.summary: Wellington's most refined dining experience. The degustation is consistently excellent. Premium pricing reflects the occasion.

pois.name: Logan Brown
pois.short: Wellington institution in a grand former bank — the pāua ravioli is legendary NZ fine dining.
pois.commentary: Logan Brown has been a Wellington icon since 1996, occupying a magnificent former Bank of New Zealand building. The grand dining room — soaring ceilings, art deco details — sets the stage for creative NZ cuisine. The pāua ravioli is one of New Zealand's most famous dishes, and the seasonal tasting menus consistently impress. The wine list is encyclopedic. A must-visit for anyone serious about NZ food.

reviews.summary: A Wellington institution for good reason. The pāua ravioli alone is worth the visit. The grand banking chamber adds drama to every meal.

pois.name: Rita
pois.short: Wellington's hottest restaurant — Mediterranean-inspired food, natural wines and a buzzy Cuba Street atmosphere.
pois.commentary: Rita has been one of Wellington's most talked-about restaurants since opening. The Mediterranean-inspired menu — fresh pasta, chargrilled meats, seasonal vegetables — is executed with flair and confidence. The natural wine list is one of the best in the city, and the staff are passionate about guiding you through it. The Cuba Street atmosphere is energetic without being overwhelming. No bookings for small groups.

reviews.summary: One of Wellington's most exciting recent openings. The natural wine list is impressive. No-bookings policy means waits at peak times.

pois.name: Hannah's Laneway
pois.short: Heritage laneway off Cuba Street — small cafés, bars and eateries in a converted shoe factory precinct.
pois.commentary: Hannah's Laneway is a charming pedestrian lane tucked behind Cuba Street, named after the former Hannah's shoe factory. The converted warehouses now house small cafés, wine bars and eateries. The laneway atmosphere — fairy lights, exposed brick, intimate spaces — is quintessentially Wellington. It's the kind of place you stumble upon and then return to every visit. Leeds Street Bakery is a particular highlight.

reviews.summary: A Wellington hidden gem. The laneway atmosphere is charming. Some find the individual venues small, but that's the appeal.

pois.name: Foxglove Bar & Kitchen
pois.short: Wellington waterfront bar and restaurant — eclectic global menu, creative cocktails and harbour views.
pois.commentary: Foxglove occupies a beautiful waterfront space with views across Wellington Harbour. The menu is eclectic and global — think Korean fried chicken, Middle Eastern dips, NZ lamb — and the cocktail programme is one of the most creative in the city. The outdoor terrace is the place to be on a summer evening, and the interior has a warm, moody atmosphere for winter. A versatile spot that works for everything from after-work drinks to dinner.

reviews.summary: Great waterfront location with creative food and drinks. The cocktails are a highlight. Some find the eclectic menu lacks focus.

pois.name: Zealandia Te Māra a Tāne — Ecosanctuary
pois.short: World-first fully fenced urban ecosanctuary — kaka, takahē, tuatara, and little spotted kiwi in central Wellington.
pois.commentary: Zealandia is the world's first fully fenced urban ecosanctuary, protecting 225 hectares of regenerating bush within Wellington city. Rare species including takahē, little spotted kiwi, tuatara, and hihi (stitchbird) thrive within the predator-proof fence. Kaka from Zealandia now roam the whole city.

reviews.summary: World-class urban conservation. The birds are incredibly tame. Night tours for kiwi and tuatara highly recommended.

pois.name: Kāpiti Island Nature Reserve
pois.short: Predator-free island fortress — kōkako, little spotted kiwi, and saddleback in pristine bush.
pois.commentary: Kāpiti Island is one of NZ's most important nature reserves, pest-free since 1996. The island supports little spotted kiwi, kōkako, saddleback, stitchbird, and many other rare species. Limited daily permits (max 50 visitors) ensure minimal disturbance. Boat from Paraparaumu.

reviews.summary: Premier NZ wildlife destination. Permits book months ahead. The birding is extraordinary. Take a guided walk.

pois.name: Pauatahanui Inlet — Estuarine Birds
pois.short: Wellington's most important estuary for wading birds — godwit, spoonbill, and oystercatcher.
pois.commentary: Pauatahanui Inlet north of Wellington is the region's most significant estuarine bird habitat. Bar-tailed godwit, royal spoonbill, variable oystercatcher, and pied stilt are regular visitors. The inlet walkway provides excellent viewing access. Best at low tide.

reviews.summary: Good birding close to Wellington. The walkway is well maintained. Low tide is essential for waders.

pois.name: Matiu / Somes Island — Wildlife Refuge
pois.short: Predator-free harbour island — tuatara, little blue penguins, and weta a ferry ride from the city.
pois.commentary: Matiu/Somes Island in Wellington Harbour is predator-free and hosts tuatara, little blue penguins, giant weta, and diverse bush and shore birds. The East by West ferry stops here, making it an easy half-day trip. Historical quarantine station buildings add interest.

reviews.summary: Wonderful day trip. The tuatara are impressive. Penguin nesting boxes visible. Easy ferry access.

pois.name: Red Rocks — Fur Seal Colony
pois.short: NZ fur seal colony on Wellington's wild south coast — up to 100 seals hauled out during winter.
pois.commentary: The Red Rocks coastal walk on Wellington's south coast leads to a NZ fur seal colony where up to 100 seals haul out, particularly during winter. The walk passes dramatic red-coloured rocks (pillow lavas) and fossil-rich limestone. The wild coast is a dramatic contrast to the nearby city.

reviews.summary: Great coastal walk with reliable seal sightings in winter. The geology is fascinating. Can be very windy.

pois.name: Miramar Peninsula — Little Penguin Corridor
pois.short: Little blue penguins nest around Wellington's Miramar Peninsula — community protection in an urban setting.
pois.commentary: The Miramar Peninsula and adjacent Shelly Bay area host little blue penguin nesting sites in an urban setting. The Wellington community actively protects nesting penguins with predator traps and nest boxes. Dusk viewing from public viewing points during breeding season.

reviews.summary: Urban penguin conservation in action. Dusk timing is essential. Community engagement is impressive.

pois.name: Remutaka / Wainuiomata — Kiwi Territory
pois.short: North Island brown kiwi reintroduced to the Remutaka Range — intensive pest control supports the growing population.
pois.commentary: North Island brown kiwi were reintroduced to the Remutaka Range near Wellington in 2017 as part of the Capital Kiwi project. Intensive predator trapping across thousands of hectares supports the growing population. Night listening from forest edges can be rewarding.

reviews.summary: Exciting to have kiwi near Wellington. Night listening requires patience. The bush is beautiful.

pois.name: Taputeranga Marine Reserve
pois.short: Wellington's marine reserve — kelp forests, blue cod, and crayfish recovering on the south coast.
pois.commentary: Taputeranga Marine Reserve on Wellington's south coast protects 8.55 sq km of rocky reef, kelp forest, and sandy seabed. Blue cod, butterfish, and crayfish are recovering. The Island Bay marine education centre provides interpretation. Snorkelling and diving access from several points.

reviews.summary: Good marine life close to the city. Best on calm days — the coast can be rough. Fish populations are increasing.

pois.name: Ngā Manu Nature Reserve
pois.short: Wetland sanctuary with kiwi, tuatara, eels, and bush birds — night tours available.
pois.commentary: Ngā Manu Nature Reserve near Waikanae is a 14-hectare wildlife sanctuary combining wetlands, bush, and enclosures for threatened species. Kiwi, tuatara, longfin eels, and native bush birds are all here. Night tours offer kiwi and morepork viewing. Educational and family-friendly.

reviews.summary: Great for families. The night tour is excellent. Good variety of native species. Well-run sanctuary.

pois.name: Makara Peak Mountain Bike Park — Bush Birds
pois.short: Regenerating bush on Wellington's western hills — excellent tui, kaka, and kereru from Zealandia spill-over.
pois.commentary: Makara Peak, while known as a mountain bike park, has excellent bush birding thanks to predator control and the Zealandia spill-over effect. Kaka from Zealandia visit regularly, and tui, kereru, and bellbird populations are strong. Walking tracks offer quieter birding than the bike trails.

reviews.summary: Good birding on Wellington's doorstep. The kaka from Zealandia are a bonus. Use walking tracks for best birding.

pois.name: East Harbour Regional Park — Forest Birding
pois.short: Regenerating forest across the harbour from Wellington — kaka, bellbird, and North Island robin.
pois.commentary: East Harbour Regional Park covers the hills between Eastbourne and Wainuiomata. Community pest control has boosted bush bird populations significantly. Kaka from Zealandia visit, bellbird are abundant, and North Island robin have been translocated. Multiple track options.

reviews.summary: Good bush birding near Wellington. Multiple track options for different fitness levels. The Zealandia effect is noticeable.

pois.name: Lake Wairarapa Wetlands — Ramsar Site
pois.short: Ramsar-listed wetland — bittern, fernbird, and NZ's largest black shag colony.
pois.commentary: Lake Wairarapa and its surrounding wetlands form a Ramsar site of international importance. The wetlands support Australasian bittern, fernbird, marsh crake, and NZ's largest black shag breeding colony. The Matthews Reserve and Lake Domain provide viewing access.

reviews.summary: Significant wetland. The shag colony is impressive. Access points well sign-posted. Best in early morning.

pois.name: Cape Palliser — Fur Seal Colony
pois.short: NZ's largest North Island fur seal colony — hundreds of seals on the rocks below the lighthouse.
pois.commentary: Cape Palliser hosts the largest NZ fur seal colony in the North Island, with hundreds of seals hauled out on the rocky coast below the lighthouse. The 250-step climb to the lighthouse offers panoramic views. The drive along the Palliser coast is scenic and remote.

reviews.summary: Impressive seal colony. The lighthouse climb is worth it. The drive is dramatic. Watch for seal pups on the rocks.

pois.name: Johnston Hill — Kaka & Tui Haven
pois.short: Wellington Town Belt bush walk with abundant kaka, tui, and kereru — the Zealandia effect in action.
pois.commentary: Johnston Hill in Wellington's Town Belt has become a hotspot for kaka from nearby Zealandia. The walk through native bush delivers close encounters with kaka, abundant tui, kereru, and fantail. The kaka are bold and photogenic, often landing on track-side trees.

reviews.summary: Easy access to kaka in the wild. The Town Belt bush is lush. Great for a morning or evening walk.

pois.name: Dowse Art Museum
pois.short: Innovative Lower Hutt art museum celebrating contemporary art, craft, and design with free admission.
pois.commentary: The Dowse Art Museum in Lower Hutt is one of the Wellington region's most dynamic and innovative gallery spaces, presenting an adventurous programme of contemporary art, craft, and design exhibitions. The Dowse has a particular strength in decorative arts and craft — jewellery, ceramics, textiles, and furniture design feature prominently alongside painting, sculpture, and new media. The museum champions diverse voices and experimental practice, often presenting challenging and thought-provoking exhibitions that push creative boundaries. The collection includes significant holdings of New Zealand studio craft. Admission is free. The museum underwent a major expansion and redesign in 2007, creating bright, flexible gallery spaces. The on-site gift shop is excellent for well-designed objects and jewellery. Located in the Hutt City civic precinct, the Dowse is easily reached from Wellington via train (Waterloo station) or by car (about 15 minutes from central Wellington). The museum café is popular with locals. Check the website for current exhibitions and regular public programmes including artist talks and workshops.

reviews.summary: A real hidden gem of the Wellington arts scene — the exhibitions are consistently interesting and often surprising. Great strength in craft and design. Free entry and the gift shop is dangerous for the wallet.

pois.name: BATS Theatre
pois.short: Wellington's beloved independent theatre — the beating heart of NZ's fringe and experimental performance scene.
pois.commentary: BATS Theatre on Kent Terrace is the beating heart of Wellington's vibrant independent performing arts scene and one of the most important incubators for new theatre in New Zealand. Operating since 1989, BATS provides affordable rehearsal and performance spaces for emerging and experimental artists, presenting a packed programme of new works across its two intimate venues. Many of New Zealand's most celebrated theatre practitioners, comedians, and performers started at BATS. The theatre is central to the annual New Zealand Fringe Festival and presents a wildly diverse programme year-round — expect everything from groundbreaking drama to absurdist comedy, physical theatre, improv, poetry slams, and everything in between. The quality varies (that's part of the charm), but the best shows rival anything in the country. Tickets are deliberately kept affordable, making it accessible for all. The bar is a great pre and post-show gathering spot. Check the programme online — shows change frequently and popular seasons sell out. A Wellington institution.

reviews.summary: BATS is what makes Wellington's arts scene special — raw, brave, surprising theatre in intimate spaces. Not every show is a hit but the best ones are unforgettable. The atmosphere and affordability are fantastic.

pois.name: Circa Theatre
pois.short: Wellington's premier professional theatre company in a waterfront location, presenting NZ and international works.
pois.commentary: Circa Theatre is Wellington's leading professional theatre, occupying a purpose-built waterfront venue on the harbour edge near Te Papa museum. Since 1976, Circa has been producing high-quality professional theatre that ranges from bold new New Zealand works to carefully chosen international plays and musicals. The company operates two performance spaces — Circa One (approximately 250 seats) and the more intimate Circa Two. The theatre has an in-house professional company supplemented by guest directors and designers, consistently producing some of the finest theatre in the country. The waterfront location is superb, with a licensed bar and café overlooking the harbour — perfect for pre-show dining. Many of New Zealand's finest actors, directors, and playwrights have deep connections to Circa. The programme offers approximately 12-15 productions per year across the two spaces. Booking ahead is recommended for popular seasons. The combination of excellent theatre in a stunning harbour setting makes Circa a highlight of Wellington's cultural landscape.

reviews.summary: Consistently excellent professional theatre in a wonderful waterfront setting. The quality of acting and production is outstanding. The bar overlooking the harbour is perfect for a pre-show drink.

pois.name: Michael Fowler Centre
pois.short: Wellington's premier concert venue with world-renowned acoustics, home to the NZ Symphony Orchestra.
pois.commentary: The Michael Fowler Centre is Wellington's principal concert venue, renowned for having some of the finest acoustics of any concert hall in the world. Designed by Sir Miles Warren and completed in 1983, the 2,210-seat auditorium is the home venue for the New Zealand Symphony Orchestra and hosts major concerts, events, and performances year-round. The hall's acoustic excellence was designed by Sir Harold Marshall, a New Zealand acoustician of international renown who pioneered the concept of lateral sound reflections. Visiting international musicians frequently praise the hall's acoustic qualities. The venue also hosts popular music concerts, corporate events, and Wellington's famous Beervana festival. Located on Wakefield Street in the civic precinct, adjacent to the Town Hall and Civic Square. The Michael Fowler Centre is at the heart of Wellington's cultural infrastructure. For the best acoustic experience, attend an NZSO concert — the orchestra-in-residence truly shines in this space. Booking through Ticketmaster or the venue website.

reviews.summary: World-class acoustics — hearing the NZSO here is an incredible experience. The sound quality from every seat is remarkable. A venue that genuinely enhances any musical performance.

pois.name: Pencarrow Head Lighthouse
pois.short: NZ's first permanent lighthouse (1859), reached by a scenic coastal walk with harbour entrance views.
pois.commentary: Pencarrow Head Lighthouse holds the distinction of being New Zealand's first permanent lighthouse, established in 1859 at the entrance to Wellington Harbour. It was also notable as the workplace of Mary Jane Bennett, New Zealand's only female lighthouse keeper, who tended the light from 1859 to 1865. The lighthouse is reached by a scenic walk or mountain bike ride along a vehicle-free coastal road from Burdans Gate car park at the eastern end of Eastbourne. The 5-kilometre route follows the rugged south coast of the harbour entrance with spectacular views of the harbour heads, the Kaikoura Ranges across Cook Strait, and passing ships. Two lighthouses stand at Pencarrow — the original upper iron tower (1859) and the lower replacement (1906) closer to the water. The walk is exposed to southerly winds but magnificent on calm days. The area is home to little blue penguins — take care during breeding season. Allow 2-3 hours return from Burdans Gate. Mountain biking the route is popular and faster. A powerful piece of New Zealand maritime heritage in a beautiful setting.

reviews.summary: A wonderful coastal walk to a historically significant lighthouse. The harbour views are spectacular and the Mary Jane Bennett story is inspiring. Mountain biking the route is great fun. Be prepared for wind!

pois.name: Scorching Bay Beach
pois.short: Wellington's favourite sheltered swimming beach with calm water, golden sand, and a beloved beachside café.
pois.commentary: Scorching Bay is Wellington's most beloved swimming beach — a small, sheltered crescent of golden sand on the Miramar Peninsula that catches the sun and is protected from the capital's notorious southerly winds. The beach faces northwest into the harbour, providing calm, warm swimming conditions that are as good as it gets in Wellington. The popular Scorch-o-Rama café sits right on the beach, serving excellent coffee and food with ocean views. On warm weekends, the beach and café have a wonderful community atmosphere with families, swimmers, and kayakers enjoying the sheltered conditions. The beach is small but perfectly formed, and the water quality is consistently excellent. Scorching Bay is connected to other eastern suburb beaches by the coastal road, and the walk around the bays from Oriental Bay through Evans Bay to Scorching Bay is one of Wellington's classic urban walks. Free parking can be tight on summer weekends — consider cycling. The beach catches the afternoon sun, making it perfect for a post-work swim in summer.

reviews.summary: The best swimming beach in Wellington — sheltered, sunny, and with a great café right there. The community vibe on a warm weekend is wonderful. Wellington's answer to a proper beach day.

pois.name: Te Kairanga Wines Martinborough
pois.short: Established Martinborough winery producing elegant Pinot Noir, with a cellar door in the village terrace.
pois.commentary: Te Kairanga is one of the foundation wineries of the Martinborough wine region, established in 1984 on the free-draining river terraces that have made this small Wairarapa village famous for Pinot Noir. The winery takes its name from the Maori term for 'the land where the soil is rich and the food plentiful,' reflecting the terroir that produces wines of exceptional elegance. The Pinot Noir range, from the estate label through to the premium TK reserve, demonstrates the complexity achievable in Martinborough's warm, dry microclimate. The cellar door is welcoming and unpretentious, with tastings conducted by staff who know the wines intimately. The Martinborough village, centred on a charming square with cafes and boutiques, is just an hour's drive from Wellington over the Rimutaka Hill Road. The November Toast Martinborough festival is the region's premier wine event.

reviews.summary: Excellent Pinot Noir from one of Martinborough's established producers. The cellar door is friendly and the tastings are generous. Great base for exploring the wider Martinborough wine village.

pois.name: Ata Rangi Martinborough
pois.short: Legendary Martinborough Pinot Noir producer, consistently ranked among New Zealand's finest wineries.
pois.commentary: Ata Rangi is widely regarded as one of New Zealand's greatest Pinot Noir producers, with a reputation built over four decades of meticulous winemaking in Martinborough. Founded by Clive Paton in 1980 using original Burgundian vine cuttings known as the Abel clone, the winery has produced benchmark Pinot Noir vintages that rival the best of Burgundy. The flagship Ata Rangi Pinot Noir is a wine of remarkable depth, silky texture, and aging potential. The cellar door, located on Puruatanga Road in the village, offers tastings by appointment, providing an intimate experience with the wines. Beyond Pinot Noir, the Craighall Chardonnay and Celebre red blend are outstanding. Ata Rangi has been a leader in sustainable viticulture, achieving Certified Sustainable status. Visiting by appointment ensures personal attention and the opportunity to taste wines that sell out quickly.

reviews.summary: A pilgrimage for serious Pinot Noir enthusiasts. The wines are world-class and the intimate tasting experience is memorable. Book ahead as appointments fill quickly.

pois.name: Dry River Wines Martinborough
pois.short: Cult Martinborough winery revered for age-worthy Pinot Noir, Riesling, and Gewurztraminer.
pois.commentary: Dry River is one of Martinborough's most revered boutique wineries, founded in 1979 by Dr Neil McCallum with a focus on producing age-worthy wines of exceptional character. The tiny production and intense following mean Dry River wines are allocated and rarely seen on retail shelves, making a cellar door visit one of the few ways to access them. The Pinot Noir is celebrated for its structure and aging potential, while the aromatic whites, particularly the Riesling and Gewurztraminer, are among New Zealand's finest examples. The cellar door operates by appointment and the experience is intimate and educational, often hosted by winemakers who share their philosophical approach to minimal-intervention viticulture. The winery's small scale means personal attention and the chance to taste wines in the environment where they are made. A bucket-list winery for New Zealand wine connoisseurs.

reviews.summary: A deeply special wine experience for those who appreciate craft and patience. The aromatic whites are revelatory and the Pinot is extraordinary. Appointment-only adds to the exclusive feel.

pois.name: Wild Kiwi Adventures Kapiti Coast
pois.short: Guided quad bike and ATV adventures along the Kapiti Coast's rugged beaches and bush tracks.
pois.commentary: Wild Kiwi Adventures offers guided quad bike and ATV tours along the rugged Kapiti Coast north of Wellington. Tours explore a mix of terrain including wild black sand beaches, river crossings, native bush tracks, and open farmland with views to Kapiti Island. Multiple tour options range from one-hour introductory rides suitable for beginners to half-day expeditions covering more challenging terrain. No prior quad biking experience is necessary, as comprehensive safety training is provided before departure. The coastal environment offers a raw, windswept landscape distinctly different from the sheltered Wellington harbour. Guides share knowledge of local ecology and history along the way. The Kapiti Coast is about 45 minutes north of Wellington city, making it accessible for a half-day adventure. Weather-appropriate clothing is recommended as conditions can be changeable. Minimum age varies by tour type.

reviews.summary: An exciting and accessible adventure on the wild Kapiti Coast. The beach and bush terrain variety keeps it interesting. Great guides who adapt the experience to riders' abilities.

pois.name: Adrenalin Forest Porirua
pois.short: Treetop high-ropes adventure course through native forest with six levels of increasing difficulty.
pois.commentary: Adrenalin Forest is a treetop adventure course set among tall trees in the hills above Porirua, north of Wellington. The course features over 130 challenges spread across six levels of increasing difficulty, from gentle introductory platforms at Level 1 to heart-pounding high elements at Level 6, over 20 metres above the forest floor. Challenges include swinging logs, rope bridges, cargo nets, Tarzan swings, and multiple flying foxes. The self-paced format lets participants choose their own challenge level and progress at their own speed. A continuous safety system means you are always attached to the wire. The forest setting adds natural beauty to the adrenaline, with native birds often providing a soundtrack. Allow 2-3 hours for a full session. Minimum age is 6 years with height restrictions. Located at Battle Hill Farm Forest Park in Pauatahanui, about 30 minutes from central Wellington.

reviews.summary: A brilliant outdoor adventure with challenges for every ability level. The progressive difficulty means you can push your limits gradually. The forest setting is beautiful and the flying foxes are exhilarating.

pois.name: Martinborough Wine Village
pois.short: Charming wine village with over 20 cellar doors within cycling distance, famous for world-class Pinot Noir.
pois.commentary: Martinborough is New Zealand's most compact and walkable wine village, with over 20 cellar doors located within easy cycling distance of the charming village square. The region's fame rests on its exceptional Pinot Noir, produced on free-draining alluvial terraces with a warm, dry microclimate that mirrors aspects of Burgundy. Beyond the flagship Pinot, excellent Sauvignon Blanc, Chardonnay, Riesling, and Syrah are produced. Bike hire is available from the village centre, making a self-guided cycling wine tour the classic Martinborough experience. The village square is lined with boutique shops, cafes, and restaurants showcasing local produce. Toast Martinborough, held each November, is New Zealand's most prestigious wine festival. The village is a 75-minute drive east of Wellington over the Rimutaka Range, or accessible via the scenic Rimutaka Rail Trail by bicycle. Many Wellington visitors make Martinborough a day trip or weekend escape.

reviews.summary: The perfect wine village experience with world-class wineries at every turn. Cycling between cellar doors is delightful. The village has real charm with excellent dining and accommodation options.

pois.name: Te Papa Tongarewa Museum
pois.short: New Zealand's national museum on the Wellington waterfront, with world-class exhibitions and free general admission.
pois.commentary: Te Papa Tongarewa (Our Container of Treasured Things) is New Zealand's national museum, housed in a striking waterfront building in central Wellington. The museum is renowned for its innovative, interactive approach to presenting New Zealand's natural environment, Maori culture, Pacific heritage, and art collections. Permanent exhibitions include the immersive earthquake simulator, the spectacular Gallipoli exhibition created with Weta Workshop, the magnificent marae (meeting house), and extensive natural history displays featuring a colossal squid. Temporary exhibitions bring international content to Wellington. General admission is free, with charges for some special exhibitions and experiences. The museum spans six floors and easily absorbs a full day. The cafe and gift shop are excellent. Located on Cable Street on the waterfront, Te Papa is within walking distance of Cuba Street, Courtenay Place, and the Wellington cable car. New Zealand's most-visited museum with over 1.5 million visitors annually.

reviews.summary: A world-class museum that brings New Zealand's stories to life in innovative ways. The earthquake experience and Gallipoli exhibition are standouts. Free entry makes it accessible to all visitors.

pois.name: Weta Workshop Unleashed
pois.short: Immersive experience at the world-famous Weta Workshop, featuring movie props, creatures, and behind-the-scenes tours.
pois.commentary: Weta Workshop Unleashed is an immersive visitor experience at the world-famous Weta Workshop in Miramar, Wellington, the special effects and prop-making facility behind The Lord of the Rings, Avatar, and numerous other blockbuster films. The experience features interactive exhibitions showcasing the art and technology of filmmaking, from miniature model-making and prosthetics to digital effects and costume design. Visitors can see original props, weapons, and creatures from major film productions, and some experiences include hands-on workshops where you can try techniques used in film production. The Miramar creative precinct surrounding Weta has become Wellington's movie-making hub, with several studios and post-production facilities. A gift shop offers unique Weta-designed collectibles and artwork. Located in the suburb of Miramar, about 15 minutes from central Wellington. Booking is recommended, especially during school holidays and peak tourist season.

reviews.summary: A fascinating behind-the-scenes look at world-class filmmaking craft. The props and creatures are extraordinary up close. A must for film fans and anyone who appreciates creative artistry.

pois.name: Zealandia Te Māra a Tāne
pois.short: The world's first fully fenced urban ecosanctuary — kiwi, tuatara, and rare birds thrive minutes from the CBD.
pois.commentary: Zealandia is a conservation marvel — 225 hectares of regenerating bush in a former reservoir valley, enclosed by a predator-proof fence and just minutes from central Wellington. Since opening in 1999, over 40 native species have been reintroduced, including little spotted kiwi, tuatara, hihi, and takahē. The night tour is the standout experience: guided walks through the dark forest with chances to see kiwi, tuatara, and wētā in their natural environment. By day, the bush walks and lake are peaceful and the birdlife is extraordinary for an urban setting.

reviews.summary: The night tour is rated as one of Wellington's must-do experiences. Seeing kiwi in a near-natural setting delights visitors. The proximity to the CBD surprises everyone.

pois.name: Wellington Cable Car & Kelburn Lookout
pois.short: Wellington's beloved icon since 1902 — ride up to Kelburn for panoramic views over the harbour and city.
pois.commentary: The Wellington Cable Car has been climbing from Lambton Quay to the Kelburn ridge since 1902, making it one of New Zealand's most iconic transport experiences. The five-minute ride gains 120 vertical metres, and the view from the top — harbour, city, hills, and on clear days, the South Island — is stunning. At the top, you can explore the Botanic Garden, visit the Cable Car Museum (free), or walk downhill through the gardens back to the city. It's a must-do on every Wellington visitor's list, and the red cars are as photogenic as the view.

reviews.summary: Visitors rate the cable car as a Wellington essential. The top-station views are praised in every review. Walking back down through the Botanic Garden is recommended over riding down.

pois.name: Mount Victoria Lookout
pois.short: The classic Wellington viewpoint — 360° views from a LOTR filming location. Best at sunrise or sunset.
pois.commentary: Mount Victoria / Matairangi offers the definitive Wellington panorama — 360 degrees of harbour, city, hills, airport, and on clear days, the South Island and Kaikōura ranges. The summit is accessible by car or a 20-minute walk through the pine forest that doubled as the Hobbits' escape from the Shire in The Fellowship of the Ring. Sunrise and sunset are the magic hours: the harbour glows gold and the city lights begin to sparkle. It's free, it's easy, and it's the best way to understand Wellington's harbour-bowl geography.

reviews.summary: Visitors rate the views as the best in Wellington. The LOTR connection adds excitement for film fans. Sunrise visits are recommended for photography and solitude.

pois.name: Martinborough Wine Village
pois.short: Compact wine village where world-class Pinot Noir cellar doors are within walking or cycling distance.
pois.commentary: Martinborough is New Zealand's most walkable wine region — over 20 cellar doors are within a few kilometres of the village square, making it perfect for cycling between tastings. The Pinot Noir is the star: Ata Rangi, Dry River, and Palliser Estate produce bottles that rival Burgundy at a fraction of the price. The village itself is charming: a grid of streets around a central square, with excellent restaurants, boutique accommodation, and a Saturday farmers' market. The annual Toast Martinborough festival in November draws thousands. An hour's drive over the Rimutaka Range from Wellington.

reviews.summary: Wine lovers rate Martinborough's Pinot Noir among the best in the world. The compact, cyclable layout is praised as unique. Weekend visits are recommended for the full village atmosphere.

pois.name: Matiu / Somes Island
pois.short: Harbour island with layers of history — quarantine station, internment camp, and now a predator-free wildlife reserve.
pois.commentary: Matiu / Somes Island sits in the middle of Wellington Harbour, accessible by a short ferry ride from Queens Wharf. The island's history runs deep: quarantine station, internment camp for 'enemy aliens' during both world wars, and animal quarantine facility. Since 1995 it has been a predator-free scientific reserve, home to tuatara, little blue penguins, wētā, and native bush that is rapidly regenerating. The loop walk takes about an hour and passes heritage buildings, gun emplacements, and wildlife viewing areas. The 360° harbour views are spectacular.

reviews.summary: Visitors praise the combination of history, wildlife, and harbour views. The wartime heritage is poignant and well-interpreted. The ferry ride itself is enjoyable.

pois.name: Space Place at Carter Observatory
pois.short: NZ's national observatory — planetarium shows, telescope viewing, and space exhibitions atop Kelburn.
pois.commentary: Space Place at Carter Observatory sits atop the Kelburn ridge near the Cable Car terminus, offering planetarium shows, interactive space exhibitions, and regular telescope viewing nights. The full-dome digital theatre is impressive, and the shows cover everything from Māori star navigation to the search for exoplanets. The Thomas Cooke telescope, dating from 1912, is still used for public viewing on clear nights. Combine with the Cable Car ride and Botanic Garden for a perfect Kelburn afternoon. The exhibitions are engaging for all ages.

reviews.summary: Families rate the planetarium shows highly. The Māori astronomy content is praised as unique. Combining with the Cable Car and Botanic Garden is recommended.

pois.name: City Gallery Wellington
pois.short: NZ's leading public gallery for contemporary art — bold exhibitions in an Art Deco building on Civic Square.
pois.commentary: City Gallery Wellington occupies a striking 1940 Art Deco building on Civic Square, repurposed as a contemporary art space in 1993. The gallery has no permanent collection — instead, it commissions and presents bold, often challenging exhibitions of New Zealand and international contemporary art, design, and architecture. The programme is consistently ambitious, and the gallery has become one of the most respected contemporary art spaces in Australasia. Free entry, with occasional charges for major touring shows. The gallery shop is excellent.

reviews.summary: Art lovers praise the bold, rotating exhibition programme. The Art Deco building is a highlight in itself. Some exhibitions are challenging — that's the point.

pois.name: Red Rocks / Pariwhero Seal Colony
pois.short: Walk along Wellington's wild south coast to a fur seal colony on dramatic red volcanic rocks.
pois.commentary: The Red Rocks coastal walk follows Wellington's raw south coast from Owhiro Bay to a New Zealand fur seal colony that gathers on the dramatic red-orange volcanic rocks from May to October. The 'red rocks' are 200-million-year-old pillow lavas — volcanic rock formed underwater and uplifted — stained by iron oxide. The walk is about 45 minutes each way along a rough 4WD track, exposed to the southerly wind. Keep at least 10 metres from the seals. The landscape feels far removed from the CBD, yet it's only 15 minutes' drive from Courtenay Place.

reviews.summary: Visitors are amazed that a seal colony exists so close to a capital city. The red volcanic rocks are described as otherworldly. The walk is exposed — dress for wind.

pois.name: Weta Workshop Unleashed
pois.short: Step inside the world of Weta Workshop — interactive experiences showcasing film props, creatures and effects.
pois.commentary: Over 40,000 sq ft of exhibition space. Interactive stations let visitors try sculpting, miniature-making and digital effects. Original props from LOTR and Avatar on display.

reviews.summary: Film fans and non-fans alike enjoy the experience.

pois.name: Wellington Craft Beer Trail
pois.short: The craft beer capital of NZ — a walkable trail of breweries and taprooms.
pois.commentary: More craft breweries per capita than any NZ city. Key stops: Garage Project, ParrotDog, Heyday. Beervana festival in August.

reviews.summary: Beer geeks rate Wellington as NZ’s best craft beer city.

pois.name: Paekakariki Escarpment Track
pois.short: A dramatic clifftop trail on the Kapiti Coast forming part of Te Araroa, offering vertiginous sea views and suspended swing bridges above the Tasman Sea.
pois.commentary: The Paekakariki Escarpment Track runs 10.9 kilometres along the steep bush-clad cliffs between Pukerua Bay and Paekakariki, forming part of Te Araroa, New Zealand's long-distance national trail. The route follows the spine of a forested ridge high above the Kapiti Coast, crossing two swing bridges that sway above deep gullies, with the surf beach and State Highway 1 visible far below and Kapiti Island prominent on the horizon. Both trailheads are accessible by commuter train from Wellington, making it a perfect car-free day adventure from the capital. The track can be walked in either direction and takes approximately four hours one way for fit walkers, with exposed sections requiring care in strong wind — which Wellington delivers reliably.

reviews.summary: Rated as one of the Wellington region's finest day walks, with the clifftop drama, Kapiti Island views, and swing bridges making it genuinely thrilling without requiring technical skills. The train-to-trailhead logistics are a particular favourite with walkers.

pois.name: Remutaka Rail Trail
pois.short: A 22-kilometre cycling and walking trail following the original Wairarapa Line railway route over the Remutaka Range, including a 576-metre summit tunnel.
pois.commentary: The Remutaka Rail Trail follows the former Fell railway — a uniquely engineered gravity-defying line that operated from 1878 to 1955 using a special central rail and brake system to navigate the steep Remutaka incline. The trail climbs from Upper Hutt through Pakuratahi Forest to the Summit Tunnel, a 576-metre bore through the heart of the Remutaka Range, before descending into Cross Creek in the Wairarapa. The engineering heritage along the route is remarkable: Fell engine houses, incline infrastructure, and ventilation shafts all remain visible. The trail connects to the Remutaka Cycle Trail network, allowing multi-day riding circuits between the Hutt Valley and Wairarapa wine region.

reviews.summary: Cyclists and walkers alike praise the Remutaka Rail Trail for combining excellent forest scenery with fascinating railway heritage. The Summit Tunnel section is described as eerie and atmospheric — a genuine highlight.

pois.name: Wellington Adrenalin Forest
pois.short: New Zealand's largest aerial adventure course, set in the native bush above Porirua, with high-wire challenges including suspension bridges, zip lines, and Tarzan swings.
pois.commentary: Adrenalin Forest Wellington is set within a hillside native forest reserve above Porirua, north of Wellington city, operating an extensive course of aerial challenges spread across eight difficulty levels. Participants clip on to a continuous safety line and navigate a self-paced sequence of obstacles — rope ladders, swinging logs, cargo nets, zip lines, and mid-air tunnels — at heights ranging from 2 to 22 metres above the forest floor. The highest levels involve exposed ridge traverses and long zip lines with genuine exposure that tests the nerves even of experienced participants. The forest setting gives the experience a natural atmosphere quite different from synthetic adventure parks, and the continuous safety system means no previous experience is required.

reviews.summary: Groups and families consistently describe Adrenalin Forest as excellent value and genuinely challenging fun, with the natural bush setting making it feel more adventurous than indoor alternatives. The self-paced format is widely appreciated.

pois.name: Red Rocks Coastal Walk and Seal Colony
pois.short: A rugged south coast walk from Owhiro Bay to a seal colony resting on ancient red volcanic rocks, with crashing Tasman Sea surf and dramatic Wellington landscapes.
pois.commentary: The Red Rocks Coastal Walk follows Wellington's exposed south coast for approximately 7 kilometres return along a rock and gravel track above the churning Tasman Sea. The red rocks themselves are ancient pillow lavas formed around 200 million years ago when molten rock extruded into the sea floor and solidified in globular shapes, their reddish colouration caused by iron oxidation. New Zealand fur seals have colonised the rocky outcrops at the end of the walk and haul out year-round, with populations peaking during the June to September pupping season when the rocks can be covered with hundreds of seals. The track is exposed to Wellington's famous southerly winds, which frequently create spectacular waves and sea spray.

reviews.summary: Visitors rate Red Rocks as one of Wellington's best free outdoor experiences, with the combination of dramatic coastal scenery, ancient geology, and resident seal colony providing exceptional value. Best visited in winter when seal numbers peak.

pois.name: Mount Victoria Lookout Walkway
pois.short: A short walk from Wellington's CBD to the summit of Mount Victoria, offering 360-degree views of the harbour, city, and hills used as Shire filming locations in The Lord of the Rings.
pois.commentary: Mount Victoria, known in Maori as Matairangi, rises 196 metres above Wellington's eastern suburbs and is reached by a network of walking tracks from the city centre in about 30 minutes of steady climbing. The summit lookout provides a 360-degree panorama that encompasses Wellington Harbour, the Cook Strait, the CBD, the Hutt Valley, and the Tararua Ranges. The wooded slopes below the summit were used as the Shire forest locations for Peter Jackson's Lord of the Rings trilogy — the scenes where Frodo and company hide from a Ringwraith on horseback were filmed on the pine-covered hillside. The walk is accessible year-round and is one of Wellington's most popular lunchtime and evening circuits for city workers.

reviews.summary: The convenience, impressive views, and Lord of the Rings connection make Mount Victoria one of Wellington's most visited hilltops. On clear days the South Island mountains are visible across Cook Strait.

pois.name: Carter Observatory
pois.short: Wellington's historic public observatory in the Botanic Garden, offering planetarium shows, telescope viewing, and an interactive space science gallery.
pois.commentary: Carter Observatory perches within the Wellington Botanic Garden above the Rose Garden and has served as the city's public astronomical facility since 1941. The observatory operates a 9-inch Thomas Cooke refractor telescope dating from 1883, one of the oldest operational telescopes in New Zealand, alongside modern instruments used for public viewing evenings. Planetarium shows in the 90-seat dome cover everything from the Maori astronomical traditions of Matariki to modern space exploration, and the Space Place science gallery features interactive exhibits about our solar system. The hilltop garden setting and the historical architecture of the building make a visit here feel appropriately cosmic and contemplative.

reviews.summary: Visitors appreciate the combination of Wellington Botanic Garden surroundings with engaging astronomical programming. The historical Thomas Cooke telescope is a highlight for those interested in the history of science in New Zealand.

pois.name: Kapiti Island Nature Reserve
pois.short: An iconic offshore sanctuary visible from the Kapiti Coast, accessible only by licensed permit, where little spotted kiwi and kokako thrive in a predator-free environment.
pois.commentary: Kapiti Island is the most significant near-shore bird sanctuary in the lower North Island, rising steeply from the Tasman Sea and visible as a dramatic silhouette from the Kapiti Coast. Access is strictly controlled by DOC permit and licensed boat operators, limiting daily visitor numbers to preserve the sanctuary's ecological integrity. The island supports the country's most important population of little spotted kiwi — a species functionally extinct on the mainland — as well as kokako, kakariki, and long-tailed cuckoo. Evening kiwi spotting experiences on the island's northern portion offer one of the most extraordinary wildlife encounters available in New Zealand. The island's landscape also carries significant cultural importance as a stronghold of the Ngati Toa chief Te Rauparaha.

reviews.summary: The combination of permit-restricted access, extraordinary bird density, and the kiwi spotting experience makes Kapiti Island one of the most memorable wildlife encounters in New Zealand. Advance planning for permits is essential.

pois.name: Wellington Waterfront Te Aro Park Walk
pois.short: Wellington's vibrant working harbour walkway from Queens Wharf to Te Papa, lined with public art, historic sheds, and world-class café culture.
pois.commentary: Wellington's waterfront is arguably the finest urban harbour walk in New Zealand, a continuous promenade from the Interislander ferry terminal at Aotea Quay through to Te Papa Tongarewa on Cable Street. The route passes Frank Kitts Park with its lagoon and fountains, the historic red sheds of the Kumutoto precinct, the Oriental Bay beach strip, and the Museum of Wellington City and Sea. Dozens of cafés, bars, and restaurants face the harbour along this strip, supported by a working waterfront still used by ferries and fishing vessels. Public artworks punctuate the walk, including the famous Bucket Fountain and Neil Dawson's suspended sculpture above the waterway.

reviews.summary: Wellington's waterfront walk is consistently praised for its combination of accessibility, café quality, public art, and the working harbour atmosphere. The stretch from the cloud sculpture to Te Papa is particularly popular at dusk.

pois.name: Te Aranui o Poneke / Great Harbour Way
pois.short: 72km circuit around Wellington Harbour by foot or bike.
pois.commentary: Te Aranui o Poneke, the Great Harbour Way, is an ambitious project creating a continuous walking and cycling path around the entire Wellington Harbour foreshore — a 72-kilometre circuit linking the city centre, Petone, Eastbourne, the Pencarrow Coast, and the South Coast. Much of the route is already complete. Highlights include the vibrant Wellington waterfront, the Hutt River Trail, the wild Pencarrow Coast with its historic lighthouse, and the dramatic south coast between Owhiro Bay and Red Rocks. The full circuit is a serious day-long ride or multi-day walk.

reviews.summary: A fantastic way to see Wellington from a different perspective. The Pencarrow Coast section is wild and beautiful.

pois.name: Mount Victoria / Tangi Te Keo Lookout
pois.short: 360-degree panoramic views over Wellington city, harbour, and hills.
pois.commentary: Mount Victoria (Tangi Te Keo) is Wellington's most accessible panoramic viewpoint, offering 360-degree views over the city, harbour, airport, and surrounding hills from its 196-metre summit. The lookout is reached by a 30-minute walk through bush-lined tracks or a short drive. The views encompass the entire Wellington region — from the Hutt Valley to the South Coast, with the Kaikoura Ranges visible on clear days. The mountain was used as a filming location for The Lord of the Rings (the hobbits hiding from Black Riders) and wind-sculpted pine trees on the summit add character. The walking tracks through the town belt connecting the summit to the city are popular with runners and walkers. Sunset and night visits offer particularly spectacular views.

reviews.summary: The best free view in Wellington. Easy to reach and spectacular at sunset. LOTR fans love the hobbit-hiding location.

pois.name: Wellington Cable Car
pois.short: Iconic funicular railway connecting the CBD to the Botanic Garden.
pois.commentary: The Wellington Cable Car is one of New Zealand's most recognisable icons — a bright red funicular railway climbing from Lambton Quay in the CBD to the hilltop Kelburn suburb in five minutes. The ride passes through a tunnel and over a viaduct with views across the harbour. At the top, the Cable Car Museum explains the system's engineering history, and the Wellington Botanic Garden sprawls across the hillside with beautiful themed plantings and walkways that wind back down to the city. The Space Place observatory at Carter Observatory near the summit offers planetarium shows and telescope viewing. The cable car has been operating since 1902 and remains a working commuter service as well as a tourist attraction. The downhill walk through the Botanic Garden to the city takes about 30 minutes.

reviews.summary: A Wellington must-do. Short ride but lovely views. The Botanic Garden walk down is beautiful. Museum at the top is interesting.

pois.name: Red Rocks / Pariwhero Seal Colony
pois.short: Coastal walk to a fur seal colony past ancient red volcanic rocks.
pois.commentary: The Red Rocks coastal walk follows Wellington's wild south coast from Owhiro Bay to a large New Zealand fur seal colony at Sinclair Head, passing the distinctive red volcanic rocks (pillow lavas) that give the area its name. The 4-kilometre walk traverses a rugged coast exposed to Cook Strait weather, with the red rocks — formed by ancient underwater volcanic eruptions — creating a dramatically coloured foreshore. The seal colony at Sinclair Head typically hosts 50-100 fur seals, particularly in winter when the colony swells. The seals can be observed from a respectful distance along the coastal track. The walk can be extended to the Karori Stream mouth and around to the Makara wind farm. The coastline feels remarkably wild for a capital city suburb.

reviews.summary: Wild coastline minutes from the city. The red volcanic rocks are striking. Seal colony is reliable, especially in winter.

pois.name: Wellington Cable Car & Botanic Garden
pois.short: Iconic red cable car to the Botanic Garden with panoramic harbour views.
pois.commentary: The Wellington Cable Car is the city's most iconic attraction — a heritage funicular railway climbing from Lambton Quay to the Botanic Garden and Kelburn lookout. The 5-minute ride delivers passengers to panoramic views over the city, harbour, and on clear days, the South Island. The Cable Car Museum at the top houses the original winding machinery and heritage cars. The Wellington Botanic Garden, sprawling downhill from the cable car terminus, features 25 hectares of native bush, themed gardens, and the Carter Observatory. Walking downhill through the garden back to the city takes about an hour and passes through a remarkable variety of landscapes.

reviews.summary: An iconic Wellington experience. The views from the top are excellent. Walking down through the Botanic Garden is lovely.

pois.name: Cuba Street Precinct
pois.short: Wellington's bohemian heart — independent shops, diverse dining, street performers, and nightlife.
pois.commentary: Cuba Street is Wellington's most characterful precinct — a pedestrianised stretch of independent shops, diverse restaurants, record stores, vintage clothing, and street art that serves as the city's bohemian heart. The dining scene spans cultures and budgets — from Cuban coffee to Japanese ramen, Ethiopian injera to craft burgers. The bucket fountain, a kinetic water sculpture, is a beloved landmark. Street performers and buskers are regular fixtures. Cuba Street Night Market (Saturday evenings) brings together food vendors from a dozen cuisines. The surrounding Cuba Quarter extends the vibe into side streets with cocktail bars, live music venues, and galleries. This is where Wellington's creative identity is most visible.

reviews.summary: Wellington's creative soul in a single street. Incredible dining diversity. Great independent shopping. The night market is a must.

pois.name: Martinborough Wine Village
pois.short: Charming wine village with premium Pinot Noir producers within walking distance of each other.
pois.commentary: Martinborough is a perfectly formed wine village in the Wairarapa, where over 20 wineries sit within walking or cycling distance of the village square. The region is famous for its Pinot Noir — the dry, free-draining gravel soils and cool-climate growing conditions produce wines of remarkable elegance and complexity. Cellar doors range from tiny single-estate operations to established names like Ata Rangi and Palliser Estate. The village itself has excellent restaurants and boutique accommodation. The compact geography makes Martinborough ideal for a self-guided wine tour on foot or by bike. The annual Toast Martinborough festival (November) is one of New Zealand's premier wine events.

reviews.summary: Perfect wine village with world-class Pinot Noir. Walking between wineries is ideal. The restaurants are excellent.

pois.name: Oriental Bay & Waterfront Walk
pois.short: Inner-city golden sand beach and vibrant waterfront walkway — the jewel of Wellington's coast.
pois.commentary: Oriental Bay is Wellington's premier beach — a sweep of imported golden sand on the inner harbour that serves as the city's summer playground. The waterfront walkway stretches from Oriental Bay past Frank Kitts Park, the Whairepo Lagoon, and along the harbour edge to Te Papa — a walk that captures everything great about Wellington. The combination of city skyline, harbour views, and accessible beach creates a waterfront experience comparable to the world's best. Cafes, restaurants, and bars line the promenade. The walk is popular at all hours — morning joggers, lunchtime office workers, and evening strollers all share the space. On sunny days, Wellington's notorious wind drops and the bay is genuinely beautiful.

reviews.summary: Wellington's finest walk — city, harbour, and beach in one. Oriental Bay beach is lovely in calm weather. Excellent cafes along the way.

pois.name: Mount Kaukau & Makara Wind Farm Views
pois.short: Highest point in Wellington with views of the city, harbour, and the dramatic Makara wind turbines.
pois.commentary: Mount Kaukau is the highest point in Wellington city at 445 metres, accessible via several walking tracks from Khandallah and Ngaio. The summit views are expansive — the harbour, city centre, Hutt Valley, and on clear days the South Island mountains. To the west, the Makara wind farm's turbines create a dramatic skyline of giant white structures against the green hills. The walk up takes about 45 minutes via the main track and is a popular local exercise route. The Skyline Track continues along the ridge to other summits. Wellington's famously strong winds make the summit experience bracing — hold onto your hat. The views make every gust worthwhile.

reviews.summary: Wellington's best high-level views. The wind turbine views are dramatic. Bring a warm layer — it's windy up there.

pois.name: Fix & Fogg Peanut Butter World
pois.short: Beloved NZ peanut butter brand's factory shop with tastings.
pois.commentary: Fix & Fogg is one of New Zealand's most beloved artisan food brands, and their factory shop on Eva Street in Wellington's Te Aro neighbourhood is a pilgrimage for fans. The small shop sells the full range of their inventive peanut butters — from classic smooth and crunchy to flavours like Everything Butter, Smoke and Fire, and Cookie Butter. Free tastings are offered, and limited-edition flavours are often available exclusively at the shop. The brand started in a Wellington garage in 2013 and has grown into an internationally distributed product while maintaining its craft ethos. The shop is tucked down Hannah's Laneway alongside Wellington Chocolate Factory, making for an excellent artisan food crawl.

reviews.summary: Peanut butter lovers and foodies are delighted by the variety and the free tastings. The Everything Butter is a fan favourite. A quintessentially Wellington experience.

pois.name: Wellington Chocolate Factory
pois.short: NZ's first bean-to-bar craft chocolate maker with factory tours.
pois.commentary: Wellington Chocolate Factory is New Zealand's first bean-to-bar craft chocolate maker, sourcing ethically traded cacao beans from Pacific and South American origins and transforming them into distinctive single-origin chocolate. Their factory and shop in Eva Street offers visitors a window into the chocolate-making process, with the aroma of roasting cacao filling the space. Guided factory tours explain the journey from bean to bar, and the shop stocks their full range including seasonal specials. Their chocolate is characterised by complex flavour profiles that reflect the origin of each bean. Located in Hannah's Laneway alongside Fix & Fogg, it's part of Wellington's artisan food precinct. A sweet addition to any Wellington visit.

reviews.summary: Chocolate lovers appreciate the craft and quality. The factory tours are informative and the tastings generous. Pair with a Fix & Fogg visit next door.

pois.name: Pauatahanui Wildlife Reserve & Inlet
pois.short: A wetland reserve on the shores of Pauatahanui Inlet — one of the Wellington region's richest birding sites.
pois.commentary: Pauatahanui Wildlife Reserve protects a stretch of wetland and tidal mudflats at the head of Pauatahanui Inlet, attracting a remarkable variety of wading and water birds. At low tide, the exposed mudflats host godwits, royal spoonbills, pied stilts, herons and shags, while the freshwater wetland behind supports pūkeko, spotless crake and the occasional bittern. A boardwalk and hides allow close observation without disturbance. The reserve is at its best from October to March, when migratory waders arrive from the Arctic — bar-tailed godwits, knots and turnstones. A 45-minute drive from central Wellington but a world away.

reviews.summary: Excellent birding in a peaceful setting. The boardwalk and hides are well-positioned. Best at low tide for wading birds. Spring and summer bring the most species. Bring binoculars.

pois.name: City Gallery Wellington — Te Whare Toi
pois.short: Leading contemporary art gallery with bold exhibitions in a restored Art Deco building.
pois.commentary: City Gallery Wellington presents New Zealand's most ambitious contemporary art exhibitions in a magnificent restored Art Deco building on Civic Square. Free entry makes it accessible, and its programme pushes boundaries with challenging installations, multimedia works, and international shows.

reviews.summary: Thought-provoking exhibitions in a stunning building — always worth a visit.

pois.name: Wellington Night Market
pois.short: Friday and Saturday night street food market on Cuba Street.
pois.commentary: Wellington's Night Market transforms Cuba Street into an open-air food hall every Friday and Saturday evening. Dozens of stalls serve cuisines from across Asia, the Pacific, and beyond — a vibrant reflection of Wellington's multicultural food scene.

reviews.summary: Great atmosphere and diverse food — a Wellington Friday night tradition.

pois.name: Wētā Workshop Unleashed
pois.short: Immersive experience inside the world-famous special effects workshop.
pois.commentary: Wētā Workshop Unleashed offers an immersive 90-minute experience through the creative world of Sir Richard Taylor's legendary special effects studio. Walk through film sets, handle props and prosthetics, and discover the artistry behind Lord of the Rings, Avatar, and other blockbusters.

reviews.summary: Mind-blowing for film fans — the miniatures and props are incredible.

pois.name: Kaitoke Regional Park — Rivendell
pois.short: Lush river park and Lord of the Rings filming location for Rivendell.
pois.commentary: Kaitoke Regional Park's dense rainforest and crystal-clear Hutt River provided the setting for Rivendell in the Lord of the Rings films. Beyond the film connection, the park offers excellent swimming holes, bush walks, and some of the Wellington region's best camping.

reviews.summary: Magical forest setting — film fans and nature lovers both love it.

pois.name: Colonial Cottage Museum
pois.short: Wellington's oldest original building, a preserved 1858 colonial cottage.
pois.commentary: The Colonial Cottage Museum is Wellington's oldest surviving building in its original location, built in 1858 by carpenter William Wallis. The small museum preserves the cottage with period furnishings and tells the story of working-class colonial Wellington.

reviews.summary: Tiny but fascinating — a rare surviving piece of early Wellington.

pois.name: Days Bay to Eastbourne Coastal Walk
pois.short: Coastal walk from the Days Bay ferry wharf to the Eastbourne village waterfront.
pois.commentary: Take the ferry from Wellington's waterfront to Days Bay, then walk the coastal path south to Eastbourne. The easy track hugs the harbour shore with views back to the city, passing through several bays and the charming seaside village — a delightful half-day escape from the capital.

reviews.summary: Lovely ferry-and-walk combination — great harbour views.

pois.name: Patuna Chasm
pois.short: A private Wairarapa limestone chasm walk near Martinborough, with stream travel, ladders and seasonal access.
pois.commentary: Patuna Chasm is one of the Wairarapa's most physical half-day experiences. On private farm land near Martinborough, the route moves through limestone country into a narrow chasm, with stream walking, ladders and wet feet often part of the deal. It is not an always-open public reserve; booking, season and safety information matter, and the walk can close when conditions are unsuitable. That controlled access is part of what keeps it special. Add it when the website needs more Wellington-region depth beyond the capital: rural Wairarapa, limestone geology and a genuinely adventurous walk within reach of wine country.

reviews.summary: Known as a memorable Wairarapa adventure for people comfortable with wet feet, ladders and booking-dependent private access.

pois.name: Stonehenge Aotearoa
pois.short: A working open-air astronomical calendar in rural Wairarapa, designed for southern skies.
pois.commentary: Stonehenge Aotearoa is not a replica so much as an invitation to look up properly. Built for the southern hemisphere, it turns solstices, stars and navigation into something you can stand inside, especially powerful in Wairarapa’s dark-sky country.

pois.name: Wairarapa Dark Sky Reserve
pois.short: A region-scale dark-sky experience where rural Wairarapa nights reveal dense southern stars.
pois.commentary: The Wairarapa Dark Sky Reserve changes the best travel hour to after dinner. Away from city light, the southern sky becomes a destination in itself, connecting small towns, vineyards and coast roads with a much older map overhead.

pois.name: Greytown Rail Trail
pois.short: A flat walking and cycling link following old railway formation between Greytown and Woodside Station.
pois.commentary: The Greytown Rail Trail is a small piece of practical travel history made pleasant. What once moved passengers now moves walkers and cyclists between village and station, with hedges, paddocks and Wairarapa quiet doing most of the work.

pois.name: Brooklyn Wind Turbine
pois.short: A hilltop Wellington viewpoint beside the city’s landmark wind turbine and wild-weather skyline.
pois.commentary: The Brooklyn Wind Turbine is a very Wellington kind of lookout: practical infrastructure turned landmark, with wind as both subject and soundtrack. From the hill you can read harbour, city, Cook Strait weather and the ridgelines that keep the capital compact.

pois.name: Ōtari-Wilton’s Bush
pois.short: New Zealand’s only public botanic garden devoted solely to native plants, wrapped around remnant forest.
pois.commentary: Ōtari-Wilton’s Bush is Wellington’s native-plant library, but alive and walkable. Collections, forest tracks and old trees sit together in a valley that makes botany feel less like labels and more like kinship with place.

pois.name: Remutaka Rail Trail Summit
pois.short: A historic rail trail over the Remutaka Range, with tunnels, railway relics and forested gullies.
pois.commentary: The Remutaka Rail Trail turns a difficult old railway crossing into a generous cycling and walking route. Tunnels, cuttings and interpretive signs reveal the engineering effort it once took to move people and goods between Wellington and Wairarapa.

pois.name: Fensham Reserve
pois.short: A small Forest & Bird reserve near Carterton, protecting lowland forest and wetland habitat.
pois.commentary: Fensham Reserve is small enough to miss and good enough not to. Its tracks pass through lowland forest and wetland edges, offering a quieter Wairarapa nature stop between vineyards, villages and the bigger ranges on the horizon.

pois.name: Baring Head / Orua-pouanui Lighthouse
pois.short: A rugged lighthouse precinct in East Harbour Regional Park, reached by walking and biking tracks.
pois.commentary: Baring Head / Orua-pouanui feels like Wellington pushed to its weather edge. Tracks cross open coastal ground to a lighthouse precinct where harbour navigation, keeper life and restoration work all meet the wind head-on.
